summary: Paul Graham argues that hardware startups are resurging, evidenced by YC’s recent batch where hardware companies outperformed non-hardware peers despite investor bias. He attributes the shift to multiple enabling forces—crowdfunding, better manufacturing and prototyping tools, ubiquitous connectivity, and online distribution—making hardware more viable for fast growth. He concludes that founders should ignore investor prejudice and pursue hardware now because the old software advantage may be temporary.
key_points: YC observed a notable rise in hardware startups, which performed strongly in a recent batch.
Investor bias against hardware lags reality; founders often see the future sooner because they build it.
Multiple technology and market shifts make hardware easier to prototype, manufacture, and sell.
Hardware has always been compelling; its weakness for rapid growth may be fading.
Founders should not let investor discrimination deter them, especially when applying to YC.
money_quotes: Investors' opinions are a trailing indicator.
The best founders are better at seeing the future than the best investors, because the best founders are making it.
Maybe the advantage of software will turn out to have been temporary.
It wouldn't be the first time something was a bad idea till it wasn't.
key_insight: Hardware startups are becoming viable for rapid growth again, so founders should pursue them despite lingering investor bias.
